title: Ropa vieja
subtitle: Cuban shredded beef braised in tomato, pepper, and cumin
excerpt: Beef brisket slow-braised until it can be pulled apart, then cooked in a bright sofrito of tomato, peppers, garlic, and cumin. The national dish of Cuba: deeply savoury, colourful, and fork-tender.
sourceNotes: Ropa vieja, Spanish for 'old clothes', is the national dish of Cuba, a shredded beef stew in a sofrito base of tomato, peppers, onion, garlic, and cumin. The name refers to the shredded meat's resemblance to torn fabric. The dish traces its origins to the Canary Islands via Spain, where a similar dish is still made. In Cuba it is typically made with beef flank (falda), which shreds cleanly into long fibres. Beef brisket is the most accessible substitute in the UK and produces a comparable result; beef shin also works well. Olives and capers are optional but traditional.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions with the sauce. Reheat gently with a splash of water. Excellent reheated.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The beef can be boiled and shredded up to 2 days ahead. Finish in the sofrito sauce when ready to serve.

content.text: Ropa vieja is made in two stages: a long boil to cook the beef through and make it pull-apart tender, then a shorter braise in a sofrito sauce to add colour and flavour. The sofrito (onion, peppers, garlic, tomato, and cumin) is the Cuban building block, and here it transforms plain boiled beef into something much more interesting. The name translates as 'old clothes', a reference to the appearance of the shredded meat.

Brisket shreds better when cooked the day before: the meat is easier to pull apart when cold, and the fat in the braising liquid solidifies overnight so you can skim it cleanly. Allow two hours at minimum for the initial boil. The beef is ready when it falls apart under gentle pressure from two forks.

items.prepNote: in one piece — beef flank or beef shin also work well

content.text: Put the {{beef-brisket}} of beef brisket in a large pot and cover with cold water. Add a pinch of salt and bring to a boil. Skim the surface foam, then reduce to a gentle simmer. Cover and cook for 90–120 minutes until the beef is very tender and shreds easily with two forks. Remove the beef and leave to cool. Reserve 200 ml of the cooking liquid. Shred the beef into long fibres, discarding any fat and connective tissue.

Heat the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a Dutch oven over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{pepper-red}} red pepper, {{pepper-green}} green pepper, and {{garlic}} of garlic. Cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add the {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{oregano-dried}} of dried oregano, and {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Pour in the reserved cooking liquid. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Simmer for 15 minutes.

Add the shredded beef to the sofrito and stir to combine. If using, add the {{olives-green}} of olives and {{capers}} of capers. Simmer together for 10 minutes until the sauce has thickened around the beef. Remove the bay leaves. Serve with white rice.

items.cause: Joint too thick; simmer not vigorous enough
items.fix: Continue for another 30 minutes. The collagen must fully convert before the fibres separate. A probe thermometer should read over 90°C.

items.cause: Too much reserved liquid; lid on throughout
items.fix: Simmer the sofrito with the lid off to reduce. The sauce should cling to the beef, not pool beneath it.

items.cause: Cumin not bloomed; not enough tomato purée
items.fix: Add the tomato purée before the tinned tomatoes and let it cook for a full minute — this develops the flavour significantly. Taste for salt, which amplifies everything.

items.cause: Overcooked during boiling; shredded too fine
items.fix: Shred into longer, thicker fibres rather than fine strings. The beef continues cooking in the sofrito, so some resistance in the initial shred is fine.

content.text: Ropa vieja is the Cuban national dish, served with white rice, black beans, and sweet fried plantain — the combination known as arroz con frijoles y plátanos. Its origins are Canarian Spanish, brought to Cuba by settlers from the Canary Islands where a similar dish (also called ropa vieja) is still made. The Cuban version absorbed the sofrito tradition of the Caribbean and the cumin that arrived with the Spanish colonial cooking. It is everyday food in Cuba and in Cuban communities in Miami, New York, and across the diaspora.
